title: AI + Design Daily - 2026-07-03
description:
Friday, July 3rd, 2026.

So much for the AI takeover. Mark Zuckerberg told Meta staff this week that AI agent development hasn't progressed nearly as fast as the company hoped. Remember when Meta laid off 10% of its workforce earlier this year to chase AI? Turns out those cuts weren't as "clean" as planned, and the upside hasn't materialized yet. Zuckerberg's banking on seeing improvements in the next three to six months, but he's also spending $145 billion on AI infrastructure this year. That's a lot of money betting on a maybe.

Speaking of AI hype, a sandwich shop just mentioned artificial intelligence 22 times in its IPO documents. Jersey Mike's doesn't make AI software. It makes submarine sandwiches. But investors are so hungry for anything AI-related that even sandwich franchises are sprinkling AI dust all over their pitches. The company couldn't even explain what specific AI risks it faces beyond a vague hand-wave. At some point, you have to wonder if we've peaked on the hype cycle.

On the infrastructure side, Anthropic is having serious talks with Samsung about building custom AI chips. The move mirrors what OpenAI just announced with Broadcom last week. Everyone's trying to reduce their dependency on Nvidia, but the real question is whether anyone can actually pull it off. Anthropic says it's still committed to a diversified hardware stack with chips from Google and Amazon too.

Meta's also quietly launching a new gaming app called Pocket, which lets you generate small interactive games using AI prompts. It's the result of Meta's acquisition of the Gizmo team earlier this year. The app's barely been announced, so it's likely still in early testing phases.

And Microsoft just announced a new operating business backed by $2.5 billion, specifically focused on getting enterprise AI deployments actually working. Amazon's already committed $1 billion to the same idea. Translation: there's huge money in the gap between "we bought an AI tool" and "our AI tool actually works."

That's your week in AI.

title: AI + Design Daily - 2026-07-02
description:
Thursday, July 2nd, 2026.

Privacy-first AI platform Venice AI just hit unicorn status with a $65 million Series A. The startup gives users access to over 200 AI models while keeping their data encrypted and untracked. They're already profitable with $70 million in annualized revenue and serving 3 million active users.

India's Bhavin Turakhia is betting $30 million of his own money that the AI era demands completely redesigned workplace software. His new company, Neo, combines project management, documents, storage, and AI into one platform—designed from scratch for AI, not bolted on afterward. Turakhia says you can't just add AI to old enterprise tools any more than you could turn Nokia parts into an iPhone.

Cloudflare just issued AI companies a September deadline. Starting September 15th, their default settings will block "mixed-use" web crawlers from ad-supported pages, forcing a separation between search crawlers and AI training crawlers. The move signals that website owners want their content discoverable but compensated—and Cloudflare's calling out Google for having unfair access to more training data through its search dominance.

On the design side: AI personality is emerging as a critical design problem. Right now it's an accidental byproduct of training and alignment, but researchers argue it should be deliberately designed like any other interface element. The warmth, hedging, and eagerness of AI assistants all shape how users feel—yet nobody's actually choosing these traits.

And Google's Gemini Spark agent is now available on Mac, with new integrations for Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, and Instacart. The expansion lets Spark handle multi-step workflows across your files and connected services.

That's your Thursday briefing.
